skill.name: image-processing
skill.description: Batch-convert and compress local images with Pillow; use when you need an offline, scriptable pipeline for directory-based processing.
skill.summary: Batch-convert and compress local images with Pillow; use when you need an offline, scriptable pipeline for directory-based processing.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 46 static analysis findings are false positives. Weak cryptographic algorithm detections were triggered by image compression parameters in Pillow save operations and the word 'compress' in documentation, not cryptographic operations. Shell backtick execution detections were triggered by markdown fenced code blocks showing example commands, not actual code execution. The skill is a legitimate offline image conversion utility with no malicious intent, data exfiltration, or prompt injection attempts detected.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm pattern (false positive)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'compress' and image quality parameters as cryptographic algorithm usage. These are Pillow image compression parameters in convert_images.py and documentation text. No cryptographic operations are performed.

low_findings.title: Shell backtick execution pattern (false positive)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged markdown fenced code blocks as Ruby shell backtick execution. These are documentation examples showing pip install and python script invocations. No actual command execution occurs from these markdown blocks.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance pattern (false positive)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged normal JSON text ('PASS', 'scope', 'security') in the audit result file as system reconnaissance indicators. These are standard evaluation result labels, not probing behavior.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL pattern (false positive)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged a GitHub source attribution URL in SKILL.md documentation. This is the legitimate source repository URL for the skill, not a malicious network endpoint.

content.user_title: Convert and compress images in batch with Pillow
content.value_statement: Manual image conversion across large directories is slow and error-prone. This skill provides a repeatable, offline pipeline that preserves folder structure while converting images to optimized formats.
actual_capabilities: Batch convert images between common formats using Pillow (WebP, JPEG, PNG, TIFF, GIF, BMP)
Configure output format, quality (1-100), and compression level as command-line parameters
Recursively scan and process subdirectories while preserving the source folder structure
Prevent accidental file overwrites with a configurable overwrite policy
Report processing summary including converted count, skipped files, and error tally
limitations: Only supports local file system operations with no cloud storage or remote URL integration
Limited to image formats supported by Pillow; does not handle RAW, SVG, or vector formats
Quality parameter effects vary by output format and are not normalized across formats
Cannot process password-protected or corrupted image files

use_cases.title: Web asset optimization
use_cases.description: Convert a folder of PNG and JPEG images to WebP format with quality 80 for website deployment. The recursive option preserves directory structure for organized asset management.
use_cases.target_user: Web developers preparing optimized images for production deployment

use_cases.title: Media library standardization
use_cases.description: Normalize a mixed-format photo collection to JPEG format with consistent quality settings. The overwrite policy prevents accidental loss of original files.
use_cases.target_user: Content creators and photographers organizing large image libraries

use_cases.title: CI/CD pipeline integration
use_cases.description: Automate image compression as part of a build pipeline to reduce artifact sizes. The script exits with a summary report suitable for log collection and monitoring.
use_cases.target_user: DevOps engineers automating image optimization in build workflows

prompt_templates.title: Basic image conversion
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert all images in the folder ./images to WebP format with quality 80 and save them to ./output.
prompt_templates.scenario: Simple batch conversion of an image directory

prompt_templates.title: Recursive conversion with custom quality
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert all images in ./photos and its subdirectories to JPEG format with quality 90, preserving the folder structure in ./converted-photos.
prompt_templates.scenario: Processing nested folder structures with format change

prompt_templates.title: Overwrite protection test
prompt_templates.prompt: Test convert images from ./source to ./target in PNG format with quality 75. Do not overwrite any files that already exist in the target directory.
prompt_templates.scenario: Safe trial run before full batch processing

prompt_templates.title: Full pipeline with error handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a complete pipeline: convert all images from ./raw-photos to ./processed-photos in WebP format at quality 85. Enable recursive scanning and allow overwrites. After conversion, report how many files succeeded, were skipped, or failed.
prompt_templates.scenario: Production batch processing with comprehensive reporting

output_examples.input: Convert all images in ./images to WebP format at quality 80, saving to ./webp-output
output_examples.output: Summary report listing converted, skipped, and errored files. Each converted image is saved in WebP format at the matching relative path under the output directory.

output_examples.input: Convert images from ./photos recursively to JPEG format at quality 85 without overwriting existing files
output_examples.output: A processing summary showing which files were converted (new outputs), which were skipped (already exist), and any errors encountered.

best_practices: Always run a test batch without the overwrite flag first to verify the output directory structure and format match your expectations.
Use WebP format for web distribution to achieve the best balance of visual quality and file size reduction.
Process a small sample of images first to confirm the quality setting produces acceptable results for your use case.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill to process images from cloud storage or remote URLs since it only supports local file system paths.
Do not assume the quality parameter has the same visual effect across different output formats, as each format applies it differently.
Do not interrupt the script mid-execution on large directories, as partially converted files may be left in an inconsistent state.

faq.question: What image formats can this skill convert?
faq.answer: It supports JPEG, PNG, WebP, TIFF, GIF, and BMP as both input and output formats using the Pillow library.

faq.question: Does this skill work with files in subdirectories?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the recursive flag to process images in all subdirectories. The folder structure is preserved in the output directory.

faq.question: Will this skill overwrite my original source files?
faq.answer: No. The skill reads from the source directory and writes to a separate output directory. Your original files are never modified.

faq.question: What happens when some files fail to convert?
faq.answer: The skill continues processing remaining files. A summary at the end reports the total converted, skipped, and errored counts.

faq.question: Does this skill require an internet connection?
faq.answer: No. All image processing is performed locally using the installed Pillow library with no network calls.

faq.question: How do I control the output file size and quality?
faq.answer: Use the quality parameter with a value from 1 to 100. Higher values produce better quality but larger file sizes. Default is 80.
